_meta.description: Master English strings for ansteyapps.com. Keys are used as data-i18n attributes.

common.wellness_disclaimer: Wellness support tool — not a medical device. Does not diagnose, treat, or cure any condition. Always follow your clinician's guidance.

homepage.page_title: Anstey Apps — Rehabilitation tools for stroke recovery
homepage.hero_description: Evidence-based visual field and handwriting exercises for adults recovering from stroke and neurological injury. Designed for home use alongside professional care.
homepage.hero_languages_note: Available in 16 languages including Welsh, Canadian French and Arabic — because after a stroke, practising in your first language matters.
homepage.apps_section_subtitle: Two tools, built for real rehabilitation.
homepage.apps_section_description: Each app addresses a specific post-stroke difficulty — visual field loss and handwriting — with exercises grounded in published research.
homepage.ss_description: Eight evidence-based exercises covering hemianopia, quadrantanopia, visual neglect and scotoma. Ten adaptive difficulty levels. Progress tracking you can share with your therapist.
homepage.ss_nice: Aligned with NICE NG236 recommendations for eye movement therapy after stroke-related visual field loss.
homepage.ss_lite_detail: Try all 8 exercises in the online demo before purchasing.
homepage.rw_description: Guided tracing practice for letters, words and sentences — with language-specific content for Welsh, Arabic, Japanese kanji, Chinese characters and more.
homepage.rw_unique: The first dedicated adult post-stroke handwriting rehabilitation app available in Welsh and Canadian French.
homepage.rw_lite_detail: Try letter and word tracing in the online demo before purchasing.
homepage.clinical_section_description: These aren't wellness apps dressed up in clinical language. The exercises are drawn directly from decades of rehabilitation research.
homepage.clinical_ss: Hemianopia exercises follow the compensatory saccadic scanning approach supported by NICE NG236 and four decades of research by Zihl, Pambakian, Rowe and others.
homepage.clinical_rw: Structured tracing practice using principles from the graphomotor rehabilitation literature, with language-specific content so people practise in the script they actually use.
homepage.clinical_disclaimer: These are wellness support tools — not medical devices. They complement, rather than replace, the care of your neuro-optometrist, orthoptist, occupational therapist or rehabilitation team.
homepage.diff_languages_text: Not machine-translated English — real words, phrases and sentences in each language.
homepage.diff_evidence_text: Grounded in saccadic scanning, smooth pursuit and graphomotor research.
homepage.diff_adults_text: Adult vocabulary, adult design. Not repurposed children's apps.
homepage.diff_privacy_text: No accounts, no data collection. Everything stays on your device.
homepage.diff_offline_text: Install once, practise anywhere — no internet needed after download.
homepage.founder_title: Built for how recovery actually works.
homepage.founder_story_1: Anstey Apps was founded in 2026 after my partner had a stroke that caused significant visual field loss. The existing tools were expensive, clinically opaque, or designed for children.
homepage.founder_story_2: They were also all in English, or one additional language. These apps exist to fill that gap — real rehabilitation exercises, in the languages people actually speak, at a price families can afford.
homepage.founder_story_3: Built in the UK, by a family dealing with stroke, for others dealing with stroke.
homepage.contact_title: I'd genuinely like to hear from you.
homepage.contact_intro: Whether you're a clinician considering recommending the apps, a commissioner exploring rehabilitation tools, or a stroke survivor or carer with feedback — I'd welcome the conversation.
homepage.contact_clinician: If you are an occupational therapist, speech and language therapist, neuro-optometrist, orthoptist or rehabilitation specialist, there is a dedicated clinicians page with evidence summaries and clinical feedback channels.

stroke_sight.hero_description: Eight evidence-based exercises for the four most common post-stroke visual and attentional conditions. Grounded in saccadic scanning and smooth pursuit training traditions. Aligned with NICE NG236 recommendations for eye movement therapy.
stroke_sight.exercises_subtitle: Each condition has a distinct clinical evidence base. The exercises are designed to match — not a single exercise template applied to everything.
stroke_sight.ex_anchor_desc: Saccadic eye movement training into the affected visual field
stroke_sight.ex_reading_desc: Visual scanning practice for reading, rebuilding line-tracking patterns
stroke_sight.ex_flicker_desc: Low-demand peripheral awareness stimulation based on flicker research
stroke_sight.ex_grid_desc: Systematic scanning of symbol arrays, building compensatory search strategies
stroke_sight.ex_pursuit_desc: Slow tracking toward the neglected side — the approach with the strongest evidence for neglect recovery
stroke_sight.ex_bisection_desc: Spatial bias calibration that feeds into Smooth Pursuit difficulty
stroke_sight.ex_blindspot_desc: Awareness training around the blind spot area
stroke_sight.ex_gapfill_desc: Perceptual completion practice, exercising the brain's ability to interpret incomplete visual information
stroke_sight.why_saccadic: Saccadic scanning for hemianopia — grounded in Pambakian et al., Zihl et al.
stroke_sight.why_pursuit: Smooth pursuit for neglect — recommended by German Neurological Society
stroke_sight.why_scotoma: Awareness training for scotoma — not PRL or eccentric viewing
stroke_sight.who_subtitle: Built for stroke survivors, supported by clinicians
stroke_sight.who_survivors: Stroke survivors with visual field loss
stroke_sight.who_survivors_desc: Hemianopia, quadrantanopia, or scotoma — all four conditions are supported with condition-specific exercises
stroke_sight.who_neglect_desc: Spatial inattention exercises using the smooth pursuit approach with the strongest evidence base
stroke_sight.who_therapists_desc: A structured home practice tool to recommend between clinical sessions, with progress tracking you can review together
stroke_sight.who_carers_desc: A guided, supportive way to practise that doesn't require clinical training
stroke_sight.also_neglect: Also available for neglect, training systematic scanning
stroke_sight.expect_protocol: Evidence-based protocols suggest 15–30 minutes daily, at least 5 days per week, for 4–6 weeks. Two shorter sessions per day may be more effective than one longer session.
stroke_sight.expect_detail: You may notice improvements in how confidently you scan your surroundings, how easily you read, and how aware you are of objects on your affected side.
stroke_sight.expect_disclaimer: Stroke Sight is a wellness support tool — not a medical device. It does not diagnose, treat, or cure any medical condition. Always follow the guidance of your neuro-optometrist, stroke rehabilitation team, or other qualified clinician.
stroke_sight.why_not_generic_text: General brain-training apps target cognition broadly. They do not address the specific visual and attentional impairments caused by stroke. Stroke Sight's exercises are designed for these conditions, grounded in published rehabilitation research.
stroke_sight.features_title: Built for how recovery actually works
stroke_sight.feat_languages_text: English, Spanish, French, German, Italian, Portuguese, Welsh, Japanese, Korean, Hindi, Arabic, Chinese, Polish — plus Latin American Spanish, Brazilian Portuguese and Canadian French.
stroke_sight.feat_conditions_text: Hemianopia, quadrantanopia, visual neglect and scotoma — each with condition-specific exercises.
stroke_sight.feat_levels_text: Start gently and increase challenge as you improve
stroke_sight.feat_audio: Stereo sound that moves with visual targets, drawing on multisensory rehabilitation principles.
stroke_sight.feat_audio_toggle: Three-level audio toggle — off, subtle, prominent — for users with hyperacusis or sound sensitivity.
stroke_sight.feat_progress_text: View your accuracy and reaction times over time
stroke_sight.feat_share_text: Share results with your therapist via email — uses your own mail app, no data leaves your device.
stroke_sight.evidence_intro: Stroke Sight's exercises are grounded in techniques studied in published clinical research on post-stroke visual rehabilitation.
stroke_sight.evidence_pambakian: Pambakian, A.L.M. et al. (2004). "Saccadic visual search training: a treatment for patients with homonymous hemianopia." Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery & Psychiatry.
stroke_sight.evidence_review: Review of current evidence for visual training.
stroke_sight.evidence_raninen: Raninen, A. et al. (2006). "Improvement of visual field defects after training in patients with chronic occipital stroke." Restorative Neurology and Neuroscience.
stroke_sight.evidence_gupta: Gupta, A. et al. (2024). "Efficacy of rehabilitative techniques for visual field deficits post stroke." Clinical Rehabilitation.
stroke_sight.evidence_search: The SEARCH trial (Rowe et al., 2025) found equivalent benefit from structured practice at home versus face-to-face clinical training — supporting the value of well-designed self-guided tools.
stroke_sight.evidence_note: These papers inform the training approach — Stroke Sight is not itself a clinically validated tool.
stroke_sight.faq_medical_q: Is Stroke Sight a medical device?
stroke_sight.faq_medical_a: No. Stroke Sight is a wellness support tool designed to complement professional care. It does not diagnose, treat, or cure any condition.
stroke_sight.faq_conditions_a: Stroke Sight covers four post-stroke visual and attentional conditions: hemianopia, quadrantanopia, visual neglect and scotoma.
stroke_sight.faq_neglect_q: Does it work for visual neglect?
stroke_sight.faq_neglect_a: Yes. The Smooth Pursuit exercise uses slow tracking toward the neglected side with the strongest evidence base for neglect recovery.
stroke_sight.faq_side_q: Does it work for left and right side?
stroke_sight.faq_side_a: Yes. When you first open the app, you choose which side is affected. All exercises adapt accordingly.
stroke_sight.faq_session_q: How long should each session be?
stroke_sight.faq_session_a: 10–15 minutes is ideal. The app recommends exercises based on your energy level — shorter sessions on tired days.
stroke_sight.faq_therapist_q: Can my therapist see my progress?
stroke_sight.faq_therapist_a: Yes — the app has an email progress report feature that generates a summary you can send to your therapist directly from the app.
stroke_sight.faq_internet_a: Only for the initial download. After that, everything works offline — on hospital Wi-Fi, at home, or wherever you practise.
stroke_sight.faq_subscription_a: No. £29.99 one-time purchase. No subscription, no in-app purchases, no ads.
stroke_sight.pricing_disclaimer: Wellness support tool — not a medical device.
stroke_sight.testimonials_placeholder: Reviews and clinician feedback will appear here as they arrive. If you've used Stroke Sight, we'd love to hear from you —
stroke_sight.clinician_text: A one-page evidence summary is available for circulation in clinical teams. If you'd like a copy, or to discuss the approach, please get in touch.

rewrite.hero_description: Guided tracing practice for letters, words and sentences. Post-stroke handwriting rehabilitation designed for adults relearning a skill they once had — with real language-specific content in 16 languages.
rewrite.how_description: Guided tracing practice for letters, words and sentences — with language-specific content for the scripts people actually write in.
rewrite.step1_desc: Trace individual letters with on-screen guides showing stroke order and direction. Language-specific: Welsh, Arabic, Japanese kanji, Chinese characters and more.
rewrite.step2_desc: Common words in each language — not English words translated. Welsh words for Welsh users. Arabic words for Arabic users.
rewrite.step3_desc: Full sentences at your own pace, building towards connected writing
rewrite.step4_desc: Accuracy scores and session history you can share with your therapist
rewrite.language_title: Built for the language you actually write in
rewrite.language_text: Most handwriting apps teach children to write for the first time. ReWrite is built for adults recovering from stroke or neurological conditions, with real content in 16 languages.
rewrite.who_subtitle: Built for stroke survivors, supported by clinicians
rewrite.who_survivors_desc: Whether you're rebuilding fine motor control or regaining confidence with a pen
rewrite.who_therapists_desc: A structured home practice tool to recommend between clinical sessions, with progress tracking you can review together
rewrite.who_neuro_desc: Including brain injury, Parkinson's, and other conditions affecting handwriting
rewrite.who_carers_desc: A supportive, guided way to practise that doesn't require clinical training
rewrite.expect_text: Handwriting recovery takes time and consistent practice. Most rehabilitation programmes recommend daily practice of 10–15 minutes.
rewrite.expect_detail: Start with individual letters, where guides show you the correct stroke order and direction. As your control improves, move to words and then full sentences.
rewrite.why_not_children_text: Children's handwriting apps teach letter formation to children learning to write for the first time. ReWrite is built for adults who already know how to write and are rebuilding that skill after stroke.
rewrite.why_content: ReWrite uses adult vocabulary and sentence content, supports 16 languages including Chinese and Japanese character tracing, and tracks progress over time.
rewrite.features_title: Built for how recovery actually works
rewrite.feat_languages_text: Language-specific letter sets, words and sentences. Not just translated menus
rewrite.feat_cjk: Full support for Japanese and Chinese character tracing with correct stroke order
rewrite.feat_adaptive: Control guide visibility, letter size, and complexity to match your current ability
rewrite.feat_scoring: Designed to encourage practice, not penalise imperfection
rewrite.feat_dark: Comfortable practice in low-light settings or for users with light sensitivity
rewrite.evidence_intro: ReWrite's approach is grounded in established principles of motor rehabilitation and occupational therapy.
rewrite.evidence_langhorne: Langhorne, P., Bernhardt, J. & Kwakkel, G. (2011). "Stroke rehabilitation." The Lancet, 377(9778), 1693–1702. Establishes the evidence base for repetitive, task-specific motor practice.
rewrite.evidence_yancosek: Yancosek, K.E. & Howell, D. (2009). "A narrative review of dexterity assessments." Journal of Hand Therapy, 22(3), 258–270. Reviews motor assessment and training approaches for fine motor recovery.
rewrite.evidence_veerbeek: Veerbeek, J.M. et al. (2014). "What is the evidence for physical therapy poststroke?" PLoS ONE, 9(2). Comprehensive evidence review supporting repetitive practice for motor recovery.
rewrite.evidence_summary: ReWrite provides structured tracing practice using principles from the graphomotor rehabilitation literature, with language-specific content so people practise in the script they actually use.
rewrite.evidence_note: These studies inform the training approach — ReWrite is not itself a clinically validated tool.
rewrite.faq_medical_a: No. ReWrite is a wellness support tool designed to complement professional rehabilitation. It does not diagnose, treat, or cure any condition.
rewrite.faq_language_q: Is the content really different in each language?
rewrite.faq_language_a: Yes. Welsh users trace Welsh letters and write Welsh words. Japanese users practise kanji. Arabic users write in Arabic script. Each language has its own real content.
rewrite.faq_cjk_q: What languages does it support for character tracing?
rewrite.faq_cjk_a: All 16 languages, including full Chinese and Japanese character support with correct stroke order guidance.
rewrite.faq_therapist_q: Can I share my progress with my therapist?
rewrite.faq_therapist_a: Yes — ReWrite has an email progress report feature that generates a summary you can send to your therapist directly from the app.
rewrite.faq_subscription_a: No. £29.99 one-time purchase. No subscription, no in-app purchases, no ads.
rewrite.faq_offline_a: Yes. After the initial download, everything works without an internet connection.
rewrite.faq_tablet_q: Can I use it on a tablet?
rewrite.faq_tablet_a: Yes. ReWrite works on phones and tablets. A larger screen can make tracing easier, especially early in recovery.
rewrite.pricing_disclaimer: Wellness support tool — not a medical device.
rewrite.testimonials_placeholder: Reviews and clinician feedback will appear here as they arrive. If you've used ReWrite, we'd love to hear from you —
rewrite.clinician_text: A one-page evidence summary is available for circulation in clinical teams. If you'd like a copy, or to discuss the approach, please get in touch.

clinicians.hero_subtitle: For occupational therapists, orthoptists & rehabilitation specialists
clinicians.hero_description: Evidence basis, how to recommend, and clinical feedback channels for Stroke Sight and ReWrite — rehabilitation support tools designed for home use alongside professional care.
clinicians.evidence_intro: Both tools are grounded in published rehabilitation research. Neither claims to be a clinically validated tool — they are wellness support tools designed for structured home practice between clinical sessions.
clinicians.ss_evidence_text: Stroke Sight's hemianopia exercises follow the compensatory saccadic scanning approach supported by NICE NG236.
clinicians.ss_pambakian: Pambakian et al. (2004) — saccadic training, JNNP
clinicians.ss_zihl: Zihl et al. (2021) — 5-year follow-up, 40% improvement in compensatory scanning
clinicians.ss_rowe: Rowe et al. (2025) — SEARCH trial, structured practice benefit
clinicians.ss_neglect: The neglect exercises use smooth pursuit eye movement training — recommended by the German Neurological Society guidelines.
clinicians.ss_scotoma: Scotoma exercises provide awareness training around the blind spot area.
clinicians.ss_nice_detail: NICE NG236 recommends eye movement therapy for stroke-related visual field loss.
clinicians.rw_evidence_text: ReWrite provides structured tracing practice using principles from the graphomotor rehabilitation literature.
clinicians.rw_langhorne: Langhorne, Bernhardt & Kwakkel (2011) — Stroke rehabilitation, The Lancet
clinicians.rw_veerbeek: Veerbeek et al. (2014) — systematic review supporting repetitive practice for upper limb motor recovery, PLoS ONE
clinicians.rw_yancosek: Yancosek & Howell (2009) — fine motor assessment and rehabilitation, Journal of Hand Therapy
clinicians.rw_session: Session length is designed around typical post-stroke fatigue windows.
clinicians.rw_language_detail: ReWrite is the first dedicated adult post-stroke handwriting rehabilitation app available in Welsh and Canadian French.
clinicians.recommend_subtitle: Demo available for patients to try
clinicians.recommend_text: Both apps have online demos. Patients can try the core exercises before any purchase decision — no account required.
clinicians.recommend_ss_lite: All 8 exercises included, 3 of 10 difficulty levels
clinicians.recommend_rw_lite: Letters and words included, plus three sample sentences
clinicians.ss_price_detail: Full version — one-time purchase, no subscription. Includes 10 difficulty levels, all 8 exercises, full progress tracking.
clinicians.contact_intro: These tools are built to complement clinical work and improve with clinical input.
clinicians.contact_summary: A one-page evidence summary is available on request for circulation in clinical teams.
clinicians.contact_feedback: These tools are built to complement your work and improve with your input. If something doesn't match clinical reality, I want to know.
clinicians.contact_disclaimer: Wellness support tools — not medical devices.
